title: Legs up the wall before bed
subtitle: A five-minute pre-sleep activity for moving blood from the legs and signalling rest to the body.
excerpt: A five-minute pre-sleep activity using the legs-up-the-wall position. Lie on the floor with your legs resting up against the wall for five minutes before getting into bed. Simple, physical, requires no equipment beyond a wall.
sourceNotes: Legs-up-the-wall (viparita karani) is a restorative yoga position widely used for its parasympathetic effects. Public-domain technique. Included as part of the calm-the-body theme in Day 3 of SLEEP: A 30-Day Tapping Intensive (Rebecca J Page, 2025).

content.text: A five-minute pre-sleep activity. The position is simple: lie on your back with your legs resting up against the wall. No cushions necessary, though a thin folded blanket under the hips helps if the floor is hard.

Find a clear section of wall. Sit sideways next to it. Lower yourself onto your back and swing your legs up the wall so they rest at roughly ninety degrees. Arms by your sides or resting on your stomach.
Your hips should be close to the wall but don't need to be pressed against it. A small gap is fine. The back of the legs is resting against the wall or pointing straight up.
Stay for five minutes. Close your eyes. Breathe normally. The legs will become heavier as the blood moves toward the core.
When five minutes are up, bend your knees and roll to one side before getting up. Then go to bed.
Some people notice the legs feel lighter when they stand. Others notice that the five minutes of floor-lying was itself a form of rest.

Legs-up-the-wall (viparita karani) is a restorative yoga position with a long history of use for its calming and circulatory effects. It is a public-domain technique. Included as part of the calm-the-body theme in Day 3 of SLEEP: A 30-Day Tapping Intensive (Rebecca J Page, 2025).
